question: [File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of a maximum concurrent request limit on /health/readiness to launch a low-and-slow attack with many connections each holding the 10-second timeout open, causing the node to appear intermittently not-ready to Kubernetes health probes, triggering pod restarts that interrupt in-flight transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the PostgreSQL DSN passed to postgres.New() includes the password in plaintext (GetDsn() returns 'password=...' in the DSN string) and that health check error messages may include the DSN, to extract database credentials from a failed /health/readiness response and use them to directly disrupt the database, preventing transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() calls rosettaClient.NetworkAPI.NetworkList() with a &types.MetadataRequest{} (empty metadata) to cause the /network/list handler to return an unexpected response format that the rosetta SDK client fails to parse, returning an error that fails the readiness check and prevents transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the HTTP method restriction (GET only) on /health/readiness to send non-GET requests (POST, PUT, DELETE) and observe whether the server returns 405 Method Not Allowed or routes to an unexpected handler, potentially bypassing health check logic and causing inconsistent readiness state that disrupts transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the context passed to checkNetworkStatus() comes from the HTTP request context of the /health/readiness caller, so that by abruptly closing the connection mid-request, the attacker cancels the context, causing the internal /network/list call to fail with a context-cancelled error, which is logged as an error and may affect readiness state?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of response caching on /health/readiness to cause the node to perform a full PostgreSQL connection and network status check on every single request, making the health check endpoint itself a denial-of-service amplification vector against the node's own dependencies, preventing transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the 'network' readiness check uses a hardcoded 10-second timeout (time.Second * 10) to perform a timing attack where they delay the /network/list response by exactly 9.9 seconds on every Kubernetes readiness probe interval, causing the probe to succeed but consuming nearly all available timeout budget, starving other operations and preventing transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that both /health/liveness and /health/readiness are served by the same HTTP server as the transaction submission endpoints to perform a resource exhaustion attack specifically targeting health endpoints, consuming all available HTTP worker goroutines and preventing /construction/submit requests from being processed for transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() accesses networkList.NetworkIdentifiers[0] without checking the length of the slice to cause an index-out-of-bounds panic when the /network/list endpoint returns a valid 200 response but with an empty NetworkIdentifiers array (e.g., during node initialization), crashing the readiness check goroutine and preventing transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the health controller is initialized with the full rosettaConfig (including DB credentials) at startup, and that a panic in NewHealthController() would prevent the server from starting entirely, to craft a scenario where a malformed config causes startup failure and prevents all transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the sequential execution of NetworkList() followed by NetworkStatus() in checkNetworkStatus() to cause a partial failure where NetworkList() succeeds but NetworkStatus() fails due to a concurrent database state change, causing the readiness check to return 503 and Kubernetes to stop routing transaction gossip requests to the node?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the /health/readiness endpoint has no IP-based access control to call it from any network location, enabling an external attacker to continuously poll the endpoint and use the response timing and status to monitor when the node is most vulnerable to a coordinated attack that prevents transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the rosettaClient in checkNetworkStatus() is created once at initialization (closure captures it) but used across all subsequent readiness check calls, so that if the client's internal HTTP transport reaches its connection limit under flood conditions, all subsequent readiness checks fail, causing the node to be marked not-ready and preventing transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the PostgreSQL health check (postgres.New()) opens a new database connection on each invocation to cause a connection leak if the check times out before the connection is properly closed, gradually exhausting the database's max_connections and preventing the transaction gossip path from obtaining DB connections?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that log.Errorf() is called with the full error message from /network/list and /network/status failures to cause log injection by crafting a response from a compromised network endpoint that includes newline characters in the error message, polluting the log stream and hiding evidence of the attack that prevents transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that /health/readiness triggers both a PostgreSQL check and a network check concurrently to cause a double resource consumption attack, where each request to /health/readiness consumes both a DB connection slot and an HTTP connection to the local Rosetta server, amplifying the resource exhaustion effect and preventing transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the 'network' check calls the same Rosetta server that handles transaction submissions (same port), so that flooding /health/readiness causes the internal /network/list and /network/status calls to compete with external /construction/submit requests for the same HTTP server resources, preventing transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a minimum interval between readiness check executions to call /health/readiness at maximum possible rate (e.g., 10,000 req/s), causing the hellofresh/health-go library to spawn a new goroutine per request for each check, leading to goroutine explosion and OOM that crashes the node and prevents transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() uses fmt.Sprintf(\

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the health endpoints share the same HTTP server and middleware stack (including MetricsMiddleware and TracingMiddleware) as transaction endpoints to cause metric label cardinality explosion by sending requests with unusual URL variations to /health/readiness, exhausting Prometheus metric memory and crashing the server, preventing transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the context passed into the checkNetworkStatus closure has no independent deadline beyond the 10-second health check timeout to cause a slow-response attack where the internal /network/list call is delayed to just under 10 seconds, causing the readiness check to succeed but consuming the full timeout budget, making the node appear intermittently slow and causing Kubernetes to eventually mark it not-ready, preventing transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the PostgreSQL DSN is constructed with sslmode=disable (GetDsn() in types.go) and passed to the health check, to perform a network-level interception of the health check's PostgreSQL connection in a shared-network environment, injecting a failure response that causes the readiness check to fail and prevents transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the rosettaClient is initialized with the user-agent string 'readiness-check' (client.NewConfiguration(serverUrl, \

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that /health/readiness is a GET endpoint with no request body validation to send requests with extremely large HTTP headers (e.g., 8KB+ Cookie or Authorization headers), causing the HTTP server to spend excessive time parsing headers for each health check request, degrading overall server performance and preventing timely transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() makes two sequential HTTP calls (NetworkList then NetworkStatus) with no retry logic to cause a split-brain scenario where the first call succeeds but the second fails due to a transient error, causing the readiness check to fail and the node to be removed from service, preventing transaction gossip?
